batch_complete_note: IMG014 requires regeneration (attempt 1 of 3). All other 4 images approved.

correct_scene_match.note: Ruined school classroom — desks, chairs, blackboard, light through windows. Exact match for S009.

correct_subject.note: Multiple desks and chairs, most overturned or tilted. Blackboard at front/rear. Light shafts from left-side windows.

no_forced_labor.note: School context, not labor. Civilian community memory.

no_ghost_horror.note: Dark and atmospheric — not sinister despite high destruction level

no_modern_objects.note: All furniture consistent with pre-1974 school

no_text_logos_watermarks.note: Blackboard is dark/blank — no legible chalk text visible

usable_for_slow_pan_right.note: Wide classroom with depth works well for 40-second pan

visual_observations: Classroom: EXCELLENT — multiple desks and chairs in disarray, overturned and scattered
Light shafts: PRESENT — strong rays through left-side windows with vegetation visible outside
Blackboard: PRESENT — visible at rear, dark/blank. No chalk writing discernible.

Destruction level: HIGH — debris, dried leaves, exposed damage. More extreme than gradual abandonment suggests. This is acceptable for Hashima (50+ years, typhoon exposure, known to be heavily decayed).
No people: CONFIRMED
Color palette: Warm sepia-dark tones — close to dark_documentary target. Slightly warm but acceptable.
Teacher's desk visible near blackboard — adds to educational atmosphere.
Vegetation through windows adds sense of time passing (nature reclaiming the space).
minor_notes: DESTRUCTION LEVEL: The classroom is very heavily destroyed — more extreme than typical gradual abandonment. For Hashima specifically, this level of decay is factually plausible after 50+ years with typhoon access. Director should judge whether this level serves the scene's 'quiet sense of loss' narrative or feels overly violent. If overly extreme: regenerate with 'gradual decay, not catastrophic destruction' terms.

BLACKBOARD: No chalk writing is clearly visible — the scene description says 'faded chalk writing barely visible'. The prompt asked for it but the AI didn't render it. This is actually safer (no legibility issues). Minor visual match miss.
POST-PROCESSING: Light shafts are already strong. Add film grain. Slight desaturation from warm tones toward cooler documentary target. Apply slow_pan_right at 40-second duration.
images.post_process_notes: Film grain. Slight desaturation (the warm sepia tone is slightly warmer than dark_documentary target — pull slightly cooler). Apply slow_pan_right at 40-second duration. Narration L009/L010/L011 span the full 40 seconds.

images.rejection_reason: Image reads as a NATURAL CAVE CHAMBER, not a ruined concrete building room. The walls are raw natural stone/rock. The ceiling opening has a cave arch formation, not a collapsed concrete ceiling. The floor has natural rock gravel, not concrete rubble. This fails the scene requirement (S017: 'ruined room interior of abandoned building') and risks visual collision with IMG009 (mine tunnel, ACT_II) — two cave-like spaces in the same documentary would be confusing. The corrected prompt must anchor firmly on concrete architecture to differentiate from both the mine tunnel and natural cave aesthetics.

correct_scene_match.note: S017 requires 'ruined room interior' of a building. This reads as a natural cave/underground chamber.

correct_subject.note: Natural rock walls, cave arch ceiling, natural gravel floor — not a concrete building room interior

mine_tunnel_collision_risk.note: Cave chamber appearance creates visual collision with IMG009 (mine tunnel aesthetic in ACT_II)

light_shafts_present.note: Light beams are present and well-executed — the correct visual element, wrong setting

visual_observations: Light shafts: PRESENT and beautiful — 2-3 beams converging from ceiling opening. This is correct.
Setting: WRONG — natural cave chamber. Raw stone/rock walls, not concrete. Cave-arch ceiling, not collapsed concrete. Natural rock debris floor, not concrete rubble.
Scale: large cave, not an intimate room — fails 'room-scale intimate interior' direction

Visual collision: This image could easily be confused with IMG009 (mine tunnel section) — both have cave/underground dark-space + light aesthetics. Placing them in the same documentary creates confusion about whether we're still underground.
If reframed: the light beam quality in this image is excellent. A regeneration that places those same beams in a concrete room would likely be very strong.
images.corrected_prompt: Ruined concrete room interior of an abandoned building, a single dramatic shaft of natural light entering through a large hole in the collapsed concrete ceiling, dust particles visible in the light beam, concrete walls clearly visible on both sides with cracks and weathering stains, concrete rubble and building debris on the concrete floor, chiaroscuro lighting, beautiful and reflective, cinematic, high contrast, dark concrete surroundings with brilliant entering light, film grain, intimate room-scale of a single abandoned residential or industrial room, former built interior space --ar 16:9 --no people, furniture, text, modern objects, logos, watermark, cave walls, natural stone walls, underground cave, cave ceiling arch, mine tunnel appearance, natural rock floor, bright colors --v 6.1 --q 2
images.corrected_negative_prompt: people, furniture, text, modern objects, logos, watermark, cave walls, natural stone walls, underground cave appearance, cave ceiling arch, mine tunnel, natural rock floor, bright colors
images.corrected_prompt_rationale: Key changes: (1) Explicit 'concrete walls clearly visible on both sides' — anchors architectural material, not natural rock. (2) Explicit 'concrete rubble and building debris on the concrete floor' — not natural rock gravel. (3) 'Concrete ceiling' replaces ambiguous 'collapsed ceiling'. (4) Negative prompt adds 'cave walls, natural stone walls, underground cave, cave ceiling arch, mine tunnel appearance, natural rock floor' — directly prevents the cave aesthetic that appeared in attempt 1. The light beam quality from the first generation is retained as the visual goal.

usable_for_slow_pan_right.note: Wide ruin composition with depth works for 30-second pan

visual_observations: Ruins: EXCELLENT — concrete columns and wall frames standing at ocean edge, partially collapsed
Ocean waves: PRESENT — crashing dramatically at base of ruins, high energy
Storm sky: PRESENT — heavy dark clouds, dramatic overcast sky
Light quality: Cool blue-grey — correct dawn/storm light
Exposed rebar: Present in structural elements
No people, no boats: CONFIRMED
Color palette: Desaturated cool blue-grey — at dark_documentary target
The ruins have a strong compositional presence — columns frame the turbulent water below
images.post_process_notes: Minimal processing. Film grain. Cool palette already correct — do not warm. Apply slow_pan_right at 30-second duration. Narration L023 spans this scene.

critical_gate_check.detail: The plaque surface is heavily tarnished and oxidized dark grey-black. Any inscription engravings are so worn and faded they are functionally illegible — the surface reads as heavily corroded metal texture, not readable text. No specific UNESCO language, dates, or names are visible.

Wall: Heavily weathered concrete with texture/staining — provides the 'decay vs recognition' contrast
Background: Blurred ruin structure visible in depth of field — adds context without distraction

visual_observations: Vessel bow: EXCELLENT — white ferry bow with railing visible in clean foreground
Vessel deck: EMPTY — no people, no crew, no tourists anywhere. CRITICAL GATE PASSED.

Hashima Island: EXCELLENT — the distinctive 'battleship' silhouette with multi-storey concrete blocks is very clearly recognizable as Hashima/Gunkanjima. This is the strongest visual confirmation of the island in the entire project.
Water: Calm grey, overcast reflections — correct
Sky: Grey overcast — correct and consistent with rest of project palette
Island in middle distance: Growing as zoom proceeds — correct for slow_zoom_in

images.director_note: The island in IMG019 is very recognizably Hashima in its actual distinctive form. This is highly appropriate for the OUTRO — the viewer has now learned about the island and can recognize it fully. The image serves as the final 'you now know this place' visual cue before the credits.
